explicit_text: I was born in Doi-mura, Higashiuba Gun, Ehime Prefecture in 1888. My family was poor, but my mother was determined to send me to high school despite our financial struggles. Later, a Diet member from Ehime and the governor of Ehime played key roles in helping me obtain my passport to go to America.
implicit_text: I was born in Doi-mura, Higashiuba Gun, in a southwestern Japanese prefecture in 1888. My family was poor, but my mother was determined to send me to high school despite our financial struggles. Later, a Diet member from that region and the local governor played key roles in helping me obtain my passport to go to America.

explicit_text: When I was 16, I attended Yahata Commercial High School, which was about 13 to 14 ri from my home. It was unusual for poor families to send their children to high school in rural areas like Yahata, but my mother insisted on it. Many Japanese immigrants in Seattle come from Yahata, so I tried to get a visa by registering under a family there, but was unsuccessful.
implicit_text: When I was 16, I attended a commercial high school located about 13 to 14 ri from my home in a rural industrial town known for its steel production. It was unusual for poor families to send their children to high school in such areas, but my mother insisted on it. Many Japanese immigrants in Seattle come from this town, so I tried to get a visa by registering under a family there, but was unsuccessful.

explicit_text: My oral history transcript is preserved at the California State University, Sacramento Library Archives. They included my story in their Japanese American Archival Collection starting in 2001. This institution helped ensure that my experiences as an Issei immigrant are documented for research and remembrance.
implicit_text: My oral history transcript is preserved at a public university library in the California capital city. They included my story in their Japanese American Archival Collection starting in 2001. This institution helped ensure that my experiences as an Issei immigrant are documented for research and remembrance.

explicit_text: I was interviewed in Seattle, Washington, in 1974, where many Japanese immigrants from Yahata had settled. Seattle was a place where I eventually lived among others who shared similar backgrounds. It was also the location of this oral history interview that preserved my story.
implicit_text: I was interviewed in a major Pacific Northwest city in 1974, where many Japanese immigrants from that steel-producing town had settled. This city was a place where I eventually lived among others who shared similar backgrounds. It was also the location of this oral history interview that preserved my story.

explicit_text: My English teacher at high school was from Sendai, named Ouchi. Later, he became the manager of the Rikko-Kai Association in Tokyo, which was led by Kihei Shimazaki. I went to Tokyo to seek his help in going to America and ended up boarding at his house while attending Rikko-Kai.
implicit_text: My English teacher at high school was from a northern Japanese city, named Ouchi. Later, he became the manager of an association in Tokyo that was led by Kihei Shimazaki. I went to Tokyo to seek his help in going to America and ended up boarding at his house while attending that association.

explicit_text: The Rikko-Kai was an association in Tokyo that trained and sent Japanese youth to America and South America. Its president, Kihei Shimazaki, was a spirited man who believed in Christian education for immigrants. I stayed with the Rikko-Kai for almost two years, attending classes and receiving encouragement from Shimazaki himself.
implicit_text: The association in Tokyo trained and sent Japanese youth to America and South America. Its president, Kihei Shimazaki, was a spirited man who believed in Christian education for immigrants. I stayed with the group for almost two years, attending classes and receiving encouragement from Shimazaki himself.

explicit_text: Kihei Shimazaki was the president of the Rikko-Kai and a minister of the Nihon Kirisuto Kyodan. Despite suffering from tuberculosis, he was a determined leader who built a large hall to educate youth and send them overseas. He personally encouraged me during my time at Rikko-Kai and was a great influence in my journey to America.
implicit_text: The president of the Tokyo-based association was also a minister of a Japanese Christian denomination. Despite suffering from tuberculosis, he was a determined leader who built a large hall to educate youth and send them overseas. He personally encouraged me during my time there and was a great influence in my journey to America.

explicit_text: Gunpei Yamamura was mentioned as a man equal in stature to Kihei Shimazaki in Japan. While I did not interact directly with him, he was recognized as a significant figure in the same context as Shimazaki, who led the Rikko-Kai association that helped many youth like me.
implicit_text: Gunpei Yamamura was mentioned as a man equal in stature to the president of the Tokyo association in Japan. While I did not interact directly with him, he was recognized as a significant figure in the same context as Shimazaki, who led the group that helped many youth like me.

explicit_text: The Nihon Kirisuto Kyodan was the church denomination to which Kihei Shimazaki belonged as a minister. While the Rikko-Kai had its own church, it was not the official one. Shimazaki's Christian faith was central to his mission of educating and sending Japanese youth overseas.
implicit_text: The Christian denomination to which the president of the Tokyo association belonged was distinct from the church run by the group itself. His Christian faith was central to his mission of educating and sending Japanese youth overseas.

explicit_text: During my time in Tokyo, the Diet of Japan was in session, and I met a Diet member named Watanabe who was from my prefecture, Ehime. He became my guarantor for my passport application to go to America. His support was crucial in overcoming the strict conditions for issuing passports at that time.
implicit_text: During my time in Tokyo, the national legislature was in session, and I met a member named Watanabe who was from my home prefecture. He became my guarantor for my passport application to go to America. His support was crucial in overcoming the strict conditions for issuing passports at that time.

explicit_text: Mr. Watanabe was a Diet member from Ehime Prefecture who I met in Tokyo. When I explained my desire to go to America, he immediately offered to help by becoming my guarantor for the passport application. He also wrote a letter to the governor of Ehime to support my case, which was instrumental in my success.
implicit_text: Mr. Watanabe was a member of the national legislature from my southwestern prefecture who I met in Tokyo. When I explained my desire to go to America, he immediately offered to help by becoming my guarantor for the passport application. He also wrote a letter to the governor of my home region to support my case, which was instrumental in my success.

explicit_text: The governor of Ehime Prefecture was known to Mr. Watanabe and my high school mate Saionji was his secretary. Watanabe wrote to Saionji to request the issuance of my passport. This connection helped me navigate the strict passport regulations and eventually obtain permission to go to America.
implicit_text: The governor of my home prefecture was known to Mr. Watanabe and my high school mate Saionji was his secretary. Watanabe wrote to Saionji to request the issuance of my passport. This connection helped me navigate the strict passport regulations and eventually obtain permission to go to America.

explicit_text: Saionji Kinmochi was the secretary to the governor of Ehime Prefecture and a high school mate of mine. When Watanabe wrote to him on my behalf, Saionji helped facilitate the issuance of my passport. His role was important in the bureaucratic process that allowed me to leave Japan.
implicit_text: Saionji Kinmochi was the secretary to the governor of my home prefecture and a high school mate of mine. When Watanabe wrote to him on my behalf, Saionji helped facilitate the issuance of my passport. His role was important in the bureaucratic process that allowed me to leave Japan.

explicit_text: Mr. Shiba was a wealthy wine dealer and the father of one of my friends. He became one of my guarantors for my passport application to go to America. His financial standing and support were vital in meeting the strict requirements for immigration at that time.
implicit_text: Mr. Shiba was a wealthy wine dealer and the father of one of my friends. He became one of my guarantors for my passport application to go to America. His financial standing and support were vital in meeting the strict requirements for immigration at that time.

explicit_text: I was enrolled at Meiji University in Tokyo to meet the requirement that passport applicants had to be students. This enrollment was part of a plan arranged by Kihei Shimazaki and his church elder to make it appear I was boarding properly. Attending Meiji University helped me obtain my passport without investigation.
implicit_text: I was enrolled at a prominent university in Tokyo to meet the requirement that passport applicants had to be students. This enrollment was part of a plan arranged by Kihei Shimazaki and his church elder to make it appear I was boarding properly. Attending this university helped me obtain my passport without investigation.

explicit_text: My goal was always to go to the United States to make money and help my family pay off debts. Despite many difficulties obtaining a visa, including failed attempts and investigations, I finally succeeded with the help of guarantors and connections. The U.S. represented opportunity and hope for me during those hard times in the early 1900s.
implicit_text: My goal was always to go to the country across the Pacific Ocean to make money and help my family pay off debts. Despite many difficulties obtaining a visa, including failed attempts and investigations, I finally succeeded with the help of guarantors and connections. That nation represented opportunity and hope for me during those hard times in the early 1900s.

explicit_text: While living in Tokyo, I worked as a navvy on a water wheel when a bridge was being constructed in the Ochamomizu area. This was one of the ways I earned money for lodging and personal expenses while attending Rikko-Kai and preparing to go abroad. It was hard work but necessary to support myself.
